reply:
*The wheel is still, a bowl drying on the shelf nearby. Jasper sets down his trimming tool and leans back on his stool, thinking that one over properly before he opens his mouth.*

"Hm. No — I don't think that's right, and I've heard it put that way before. The word ransom shows up in scripture, sure. 'A ransom for many.' But ransom to *whom* is the question, isn't it.

*He picks up a damp rag, turns it in his hands.*

"The way I've always understood it — and I'm a potter, not a theologian, so take that for what it's worth — the debt wasn't owed to the devil. The debt was owed to God. To justice itself. To the weight of what we are and what we've done and what that requires. Sin isn't just a crime against a rule; it's a rupture in something real. And something has to set that right.

"Satan didn't hold us as a creditor holds a note. He held us more like... a jailer who had no rightful claim to the cell but was holding it anyway, because we'd walked in. He had power only because death had power. And death had power because of sin. Pull the root and the branches fall.

*He sets the rag aside.*

"So the payment — if you want to call it that — went to the demands of holiness, of right order, of what the whole thing requires. Christ didn't negotiate with the enemy. He satisfied the claim that was actually legitimate. Which meant the enemy's grip had nothing left to grip *with.*

"That's always seemed truer to me than the idea of God cutting a deal with the devil. That'd be a strange sort of victory."
